intro: AI infrastructure and model releases dominate this week's news cycle, from OpenAI and Broadcom's custom inference chip to Anthropic's Claude Science launch — while a sobering counterpoint emerges as Zuckerberg admits Meta's AI agents are behind schedule and LLM groupthink gets called out by researchers.

stories.title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil Jalapeño, a custom LLM inference chip
stories.summary: OpenAI and Broadcom have jointly introduced a custom silicon chip called Jalapeño, purpose-built for large language model inference workloads. This is a significant move for OpenAI, which has long relied on Nvidia GPUs and is now clearly trying to reduce that dependency and improve the economics of running its models at scale. Custom inference chips are a well-worn path — Google has its TPUs, Amazon has Trainium and Inferentia — but OpenAI entering this space signals that inference costs have become a serious strategic constraint for the company. Jalapeño is designed to improve performance-per-dollar specifically for the token-generation phase of LLM inference, which is the expensive bottleneck that scales with user demand. Broadcom brings deep chip design and manufacturing relationships, while OpenAI brings the workload expertise and captive deployment scale to actually validate the chip against real production traffic. For engineers thinking about AI infrastructure, this is a reminder that the compute stack is being vertically integrated across the board — it's no longer just a cloud provider game. The move also puts more pressure on AMD and Intel to compete on inference, not just training. If Jalapeño delivers meaningfully better cost efficiency, it could let OpenAI lower API prices or improve margins significantly. Watch for other frontier labs to accelerate their own silicon partnerships in response.

stories.title: Anthropic launches Claude Science, its AI product for scientific research
stories.summary: Anthropic announced Claude Science at an event aimed at pharmaceutical executives, biotech founders, and academic researchers — positioning it as a research-focused counterpart to Claude Code, which targets software engineering. Like Claude Code, Claude Science can autonomously execute complex, multi-step tasks given high-level instructions, and it has deep access to scientific tools and data pipelines. The announcement is notable because it signals Anthropic's strategy of building vertically specialized agentic products rather than just selling a general-purpose API. Scientific research is a compelling wedge: the workflows are well-defined, the value of getting results faster is enormous, and the tolerance for agentic autonomy is higher than in consumer settings. Pharma and biotech are obvious early targets — drug discovery pipelines are expensive, time-consuming, and well-suited to AI-assisted hypothesis generation and literature synthesis. This follows a broader trend of AI labs going directly after professional and enterprise verticals rather than waiting for customers to figure out how to apply general models themselves. The product reportedly shares architectural DNA with Claude Code's agentic loop, meaning it can spin up sub-agents, call external tools, and iterate autonomously over longer time horizons. Whether Claude Science actually accelerates real scientific discovery is still an open question, but the framing is serious and the target audience has money. Anthropic is clearly trying to own the high-trust, high-stakes agentic deployment space before others get there.

stories.title: LLMs are stuck in a groupthink groove — and a startup is trying to fix it
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review highlights a well-documented but underappreciated flaw in large language models: they exhibit a kind of statistical groupthink, consistently converging on the same outputs even when asked for variety or randomness. The classic example is asking a chatbot for a random number between 1 and 10 — you'll almost always get 7, then 3 or 4, then 8 or 9. This isn't a bug so much as a structural property of how LLMs are trained: they learn to predict the most likely next token based on patterns in training data, which means they inherit the distribution of human responses, not true randomness or genuine diversity of thought. The implications go beyond party tricks — it matters for brainstorming, scientific hypothesis generation, decision support, and any use case where you actually want diverse perspectives or exploratory thinking. A startup covered in this piece is working on mechanisms to force more genuine diversity in model outputs, though the article is light on technical specifics. Approaches in this space range from temperature tuning and diverse sampling strategies to training-time interventions that reward output diversity. The challenge is that you can't just add noise — you want diverse but still coherent, useful outputs. This is a genuinely hard problem that sits at the intersection of probability theory, cognitive science, and ML training dynamics. It's worth keeping an eye on, especially for teams building applications where AI is supposed to help explore a solution space rather than just retrieve the most probable answer.

stories.title: Mark Zuckerberg tells staff Meta's AI agents aren't progressing as fast as hoped
stories.summary: At an internal meeting, Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg reportedly told employees that the company's AI agent development is lagging behind his expectations. This is a notable admission given Meta's very public commitment to AI as a core strategic priority — the company has poured enormous resources into Llama model development, AI assistant integrations, and agentic product ambitions. The gap between what AI labs are demonstrating in benchmarks and what actually works reliably in autonomous, multi-step agent workflows has been a persistent theme across the industry in 2025 and 2026. Zuckerberg had previously set aggressive internal timelines for AI agents to perform meaningful work autonomously, and the admission suggests those timelines are slipping. This echoes a broader pattern: frontier model capabilities have been advancing rapidly, but the translation into reliable, deployable agents that handle real-world complexity without constant human intervention remains hard. The challenges are partly technical — tool use, error recovery, long-horizon planning — and partly about trust and safety in deployment contexts. For engineers building on top of AI agent frameworks, this is a useful reality check from someone with enormous resources who is seeing the gap up close. It also raises questions about what this means for Meta's product roadmap and whether the company will need to course-correct its public messaging around AI capabilities.

stories.title: AI's volatile power draw is quietly stressing the electrical grid
stories.summary: IEEE Spectrum has a detailed piece on a problem that's getting less attention than raw data center energy consumption: the volatility of AI workloads is causing grid instability that's harder to manage than steady-state power demand. AI inference workloads in particular are extremely spiky — a data center can swing between near-idle and full load in seconds depending on request volume, which is a fundamentally different challenge from, say, running a factory or even a crypto mining farm. Grid operators are built around the assumption that large industrial loads ramp up and down on predictable schedules; AI data centers don't follow those patterns. This volatility can cause frequency deviations, voltage instability, and force grid operators to maintain expensive spinning reserves they wouldn't otherwise need. The problem scales with AI adoption — as more workloads move to inference at scale, more of the grid's load profile becomes unpredictable. Solutions being explored include on-site battery storage to buffer demand spikes, smarter load scheduling by cloud providers, and demand response agreements with utilities. But these are engineering band-aids on what may be a structural mismatch between how AI infrastructure is operated and how power grids are designed to handle large customers. This is an underreported story with real infrastructure consequences, and it deserves attention from anyone thinking about the long-term sustainability of AI at scale.

stories.title: Google DeepMind partners with A24 in first-of-its-kind research collaboration
stories.summary: Google DeepMind has announced a research partnership with A24, the indie film studio behind films like Everything Everywhere All at Once and Hereditary. This is an unusual pairing — a frontier AI research lab and a prestige entertainment company — and the details released so far are sparse. What's notable is that this isn't a licensing deal or a content generation arrangement in the conventional sense; it's framed as a research partnership, which suggests DeepMind may be interested in using A24's creative processes and storytelling expertise to explore questions about AI and creativity, narrative generation, or human-AI collaboration in artistic domains. A24 is an interesting choice because the studio has a reputation for auteur-driven, unconventional content — it's not a mass-market entertainment factory, which makes the research framing more credible. DeepMind has been quietly expanding its footprint beyond pure science and engineering research into areas like music, art, and now apparently film. This could be genuinely interesting if it produces insights about how AI models handle long-form narrative coherence, character motivation, or emotional resonance — all areas where current LLMs are notoriously weak. It could also be a PR move to associate DeepMind with cultural credibility. Watch for what actually comes out of this collaboration before reading too much into it.

stories.title: New serious CVE vulnerabilities spiked around the release of Claude Mythos Preview
stories.summary: Epoch AI's data team has published a striking finding: there was a notable spike in serious security vulnerabilities (high and critical CVEs) in the period surrounding the release of Claude Mythos Preview, Anthropic's most capable model at the time. This is a correlation, not a proven causal link, but the pattern is worth taking seriously. One hypothesis is that advanced AI coding assistants are enabling security researchers — and attackers — to find and weaponize vulnerabilities faster than before, compressing the window between disclosure and exploitation. Another hypothesis is that AI is being used to write code that contains novel vulnerability classes that existing static analysis tools don't catch. The timing around a specific model release is the most eyebrow-raising part: Mythos was widely reported to have substantially better code generation and reasoning capabilities than its predecessors, which would make it a more effective tool for both offensive security research and malicious vulnerability hunting. This feeds into a genuine ongoing debate in AI safety circles about whether capability advances in frontier models create asymmetric risks — that offense benefits more than defense from stronger AI. For security teams, this data point should inform your threat model updates. It's also worth watching whether this pattern holds for future frontier model releases.

stories.title: Open Source AI Gap Map launches to index what public AI infrastructure doesn't yet cover
stories.summary: Current AI, a non-profit founded at the AI Action Summit in Paris in early 2025 and backed by $400 million in committed capital, has launched its Open Source AI Gap Map — a structured attempt to catalog where open-source AI falls short compared to proprietary alternatives. The project is ambitious: it tries to systematically identify capabilities, infrastructure components, and tooling that exist in closed commercial AI systems but have no viable open-source equivalent. This matters because the gap between what you can do with frontier closed models and what's possible with the best open-weight alternatives is still significant, and it's not well-documented in any structured way. Current AI's framing of AI as a 'public option' — analogous to public utilities or public libraries — is an interesting ideological positioning that's distinct from both the open-source software movement and the commercial AI safety discourse. The Gap Map itself is versioned (this is v0.1), suggesting ongoing maintenance and updates as the landscape shifts. For engineers and researchers working on open-source AI tooling, this could be a useful roadmap for where to focus effort. For policymakers thinking about AI access and equity, it's a concrete artifact to reference. The $400M backing suggests this isn't a purely academic exercise — someone is planning to fund actual development to close some of these gaps.

stories.title: Mistral releases Leanstral 1.5, a math reasoning model for formal proof generation
stories.summary: Mistral AI has released Leanstral 1.5, a model specifically trained for generating formal mathematical proofs using the Lean theorem prover — billed as 'proof abundance for all.' This is a specialized but genuinely significant development in the AI-for-math space. Lean is a formal verification language used in academic mathematics and increasingly in software verification, and training a model to generate valid Lean proofs is substantially harder than generating Python code because the environment provides immediate, unambiguous feedback on correctness. Leanstral builds on a growing body of work in neural theorem proving, including DeepMind's AlphaProof and various academic efforts. The 'abundance' framing in the announcement suggests Mistral believes this model can generate proofs at a scale and speed that meaningfully changes how mathematicians work with formal verification tools. For software engineers, this is relevant because formal verification has long been a gold standard for critical systems correctness — if AI can lower the barrier to writing formally verified code, that has real implications for safety-critical software. The Hacker News score of 177 reflects genuine community interest, not just hype. Mistral releasing this as a named product (rather than just a research paper) suggests they see a real market for it, likely in research institutions, defense, and high-assurance software development.

stories.title: Jamesob's guide to running state-of-the-art LLMs locally is a Hacker News hit
stories.summary: A GitHub repository from jamesob providing a practical, opinionated guide to running state-of-the-art large language models on local hardware has risen to the top of Hacker News with a score of 320 — one of the highest-scoring items this week. Local LLM inference has become dramatically more viable in 2025-2026 as model quantization techniques have matured and consumer-grade hardware has become more powerful, but navigating the tooling landscape (llama.cpp, Ollama, MLX, vLLM, various quantization formats) remains genuinely confusing. Guides like this provide real value by cutting through the noise and giving concrete, tested recommendations. The fact that SOTA models are now runnable locally at useful quality levels is a big deal for privacy-conscious users, developers who want offline capability, and anyone whose use cases don't work well with API rate limits or data-sharing concerns. The guide covers hardware selection, quantization trade-offs, software stack recommendations, and practical tips for getting usable performance. For engineers who haven't revisited local inference recently, 2026 is a meaningfully different landscape from even a year ago — the gap between cloud and local has narrowed considerably, especially for coding and reasoning tasks. This is worth bookmarking.

stories.title: Hunting a 16-year-old SQLite WAL bug with TLA+
stories.summary: Ubuntu's engineering blog has published a detailed technical post about using TLA+ formal specification to track down a 16-year-old bug in SQLite's Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) implementation, in the context of their dqlite distributed SQLite project. This is exactly the kind of deep engineering content that makes Hacker News worth reading — it's a story about patient, methodical debugging using formal methods to find a bug that survived over a decade and a half of real-world use. WAL is a critical SQLite subsystem used by essentially every application that touches SQLite, so a correctness bug there is broadly relevant. TLA+ is a formal specification language developed by Leslie Lamport (of Lamport clocks and Paxos fame) that lets you rigorously model concurrent and distributed systems and check them for violations — it's famously been used by AWS engineers to find subtle bugs in distributed protocols. Using TLA+ to find a SQLite bug is unusual but illustrates why formal methods pay dividends for complex concurrent systems even when they're hard to learn. The post is also a good reminder that SQLite, despite being one of the most tested software artifacts in existence, still contains surprises. With a Hacker News score of 195, this resonated strongly with the engineering community. Recommended reading if you care about correctness, formal methods, or just enjoy a good bug hunt story.

stories.title: Performance per dollar is getting faster and cheaper: GLM-5.2 benchmarked on AMD hardware
stories.summary: Wafer.ai has published benchmark data showing strong performance-per-dollar improvements running GLM-5.2 — the current leading open-weight model according to Simon Willison's newsletter — on AMD hardware, landing at a Hacker News score of 200. This is significant for a few reasons. First, GLM-5.2 being the best open-weight model is itself news worth noting, as it suggests continued rapid progress in the open-weights space. Second, AMD hardware showing competitive inference economics is meaningful for the industry: Nvidia has dominated AI accelerator deployments, and any credible alternative that offers better cost efficiency changes the supply chain calculus for data centers and cloud providers. The performance-per-dollar framing matters more than raw performance for most production deployments — inference costs are a real constraint on AI product economics, and small improvements here compound dramatically at scale. The AMD angle also matters for open-source and self-hosted deployments, where ROCm support has historically been a weak point but has improved substantially. If AMD can capture meaningful inference market share, it creates healthy competition and potentially accelerates the rate at which inference costs fall. This is a data point, not a trend reversal, but it's worth watching.

stories.title: MSI Center vulnerability allows gaining SYSTEM privileges in seconds
stories.summary: A security researcher has published details on a privilege escalation vulnerability in MSI Center, the system management software that ships with MSI motherboards and is installed on a large number of Windows gaming and enthusiast PCs. The bug reportedly allows an attacker to escalate to SYSTEM privileges — the highest level of Windows privilege — in a matter of seconds. MSI Center is pre-installed or recommended for users of MSI hardware as a utility for fan control, RGB management, and system monitoring, making its attack surface significant: it's both widely deployed and runs with elevated permissions by design. Privilege escalation vulnerabilities in system management software are particularly nasty because they often sit outside the typical enterprise patch management workflow — end users install these utilities and then forget about them, while IT departments don't always track them. The researcher's write-up (Hacker News score: 67) provides enough detail to understand the vulnerability class without being a full exploit kit. If you're running MSI Center, check for updates immediately. More broadly, this is a reminder that the 'bloatware' category of manufacturer-supplied system utilities is a chronic source of security risk that doesn't get enough attention relative to the OS and application layers.

stories.title: EU politician investigating spyware was himself hacked with Pegasus
stories.summary: Citizen Lab has published research — also covered by TechCrunch — revealing that a European Parliament member who served on the EU committee investigating the spyware industry had his phone compromised using NSO Group's Pegasus spyware while he was actively conducting that investigation. This is a remarkable and deeply troubling finding. It means that a government customer of NSO Group — the company whose surveillance tool was literally under EU parliamentary investigation — used Pegasus to hack the person investigating it. The irony would be almost darkly comic if the implications weren't so serious. Pegasus is a zero-click or near-zero-click spyware capable of full device compromise — it can access messages, calls, emails, camera, and microphone without any action by the target. The Hacker News post linking to Citizen Lab's report scored 326, reflecting the gravity with which the technical community views this. This finding strengthens the case for banning or severely restricting commercial spyware sales within the EU and raises serious questions about which member state government used NSO Group's services to surveil an EU parliamentarian. It also illustrates why the commercial spyware market is so difficult to regulate — NSO Group sells to governments, who are sovereign actors with intelligence prerogatives, creating a nearly impossible accountability chain.

stories.title: Google ordered to pay €4.1 billion fine for using Android to block rivals
stories.summary: A European court has upheld (or newly issued, depending on which stage of litigation this represents) a €4.1 billion fine against Google for using Android's market dominance to block rival apps and services — specifically by requiring device manufacturers to pre-install Google Search and Chrome as conditions of accessing the Play Store and other Google apps. This is one of the largest antitrust penalties in tech history, and it caps a years-long saga that began with the European Commission's original 2018 ruling. Google's response has been consistent: they argue that Android is open-source and their practices benefit consumers by ensuring a coherent, secure ecosystem. European regulators have consistently disagreed, finding that Google's bundling arrangements foreclosed competition in mobile search and browsing in ways that weren't offset by consumer benefits. For engineers and product people thinking about platform strategy, this ruling is a concrete example of how 'open source but commercially bundled' arrangements can still run afoul of competition law. The broader significance is about platform governance: how much control can an OS vendor exercise over the app ecosystem before it crosses from legitimate curation into anticompetitive foreclosure? That question is live in the EU, the UK, and increasingly in US courts.

stories.title: FAA proposes allowing quiet supersonic flights over US cities
stories.summary: The FAA has put forward a regulatory proposal that would allow supersonic aircraft to fly over US cities, provided they meet noise standards stringent enough to eliminate the sonic boom problem. This is a significant policy shift. The current ban on supersonic flight over US land has been in place since the 1970s, largely killing the commercial supersonic market in America — it's why the Concorde never flew domestic US routes. Several startups, most notably Boom Supersonic, have been developing supersonic aircraft with the expectation that regulations would eventually catch up to the technology. The FAA's proposal doesn't simply lift the ban; it creates a performance standard — you can fly supersonic if you can demonstrate your aircraft is quiet enough. This is smart regulation in principle: it's technology-neutral and gives manufacturers a clear target to design toward. The practical question is whether any aircraft can actually meet the noise standard at supersonic speeds, since the sonic boom is a physical consequence of exceeding the speed of sound, though 'low boom' designs using shaped fuselages can reduce — if not eliminate — the shockwave signature at ground level. If this rule is finalized, it could accelerate the timeline for supersonic commercial aviation in the US, with implications for business travel and eventually consumer aviation.

stories.title: Google DeepMind unionization talks off to a rocky start
stories.summary: Employees at Google DeepMind pursuing unionization have described the initial negotiation sessions as unproductive, with workers expressing frustration that DeepMind's executive leadership doesn't appear to be engaging seriously with the unionization effort. This is notable because DeepMind has historically operated with a relatively flat, academic culture that's different from the broader Google corporate structure — employees there often describe a sense of mission-driven autonomy that might have reduced the perceived need for formal labor organizing. The fact that unionization talks are happening at all reflects how the AI industry's labor dynamics are shifting. As AI systems become more strategically critical and commercially valuable, the people building them are increasingly aware of both their leverage and their concerns — about how their work is used, about safety practices, about the pace of deployment. DeepMind specifically has a history of internal tension around these questions, including the famous 2023 incident where researchers raised concerns about AI safety being deprioritized under commercial pressure. A union at a major AI research lab would be a genuinely novel development with implications for how AI development norms are set. Whether this organizing effort succeeds or stalls, it's a signal worth watching for anyone thinking about AI governance and the internal checks on frontier AI development.

stories.title: Sony erasing purchased digital movies and shows from PlayStation libraries
stories.summary: Sony is informing PlayStation customers that in September, some purchased movies and TV shows will become inaccessible — removed from their libraries despite having been bought, not rented. This is the latest and one of the starkest examples of the fundamental problem with digital media ownership: when you 'buy' a digital movie or book or game, you're almost always licensing it rather than owning it, and that license can be revoked. Sony's situation appears to involve licensing agreements with content providers expiring without renewal, which is frustratingly outside individual consumer control. This follows similar incidents with Google Play Movies and other platforms, and it's becoming an increasingly important consumer rights issue as physical media continues to decline. The irony is particularly sharp right now: PlayStation announced this week that they're ending disc-based game releases in 2028, pushing customers toward all-digital — and simultaneously demonstrating why that transition should concern those customers. For anyone who has a large digital media library across any platform, this is a good reminder to check what your 'purchases' actually entitle you to, and to think seriously about whether physical media or DRM-free downloads are worth the premium for content you care about. The Wired framing — 'digital purchases are really long-term rentals' — is accurate and worth internalizing.

stories.title: IQM, Europe's first public quantum computing company, goes public at $1.9B valuation
stories.summary: IQM, a Finnish full-stack quantum computing company, has gone public on the Nasdaq at a valuation of approximately $1.9 billion, becoming the first European quantum computing company to list publicly. What's unusually candid about this listing is that IQM's own executives acknowledged significant uncertainty about the long-term commercial viability of quantum computing technology — not the typical IPO narrative. This honesty is actually refreshing and worth noting: the quantum computing space has seen enormous hype, a correction, and is now in a more sober phase where the companies that survive will be those that can bridge toward near-term commercial value while the underlying technology matures toward fault-tolerant quantum advantage. IQM builds full-stack quantum systems, including hardware, control electronics, and software, and has existing revenue from selling quantum computers to research institutions and early commercial customers. Going public gives them capital access and a currency for acquisitions, but it also creates quarterly reporting pressure in a field where timelines are notoriously hard to predict. The European angle matters: the EU has invested heavily in quantum through its Quantum Flagship program, and a public European quantum company gives the ecosystem a different kind of visibility and credibility. Watch whether IQM can maintain its candor about uncertainty as public company pressures mount.

stories.title: Device that revives donor eyeballs could make whole-eye transplants possible
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review covers a promising development in transplant medicine: a device designed to maintain and revitalize human eyes after removal from deceased donors, potentially enabling whole-eye transplants that could restore sight. This is technically very hard. Eyes begin degenerating almost immediately after death, and the optic nerve — which carries visual signals from the retina to the brain — does not regenerate in humans the way peripheral nerves can. A previous attempt at a whole-eye transplant resulted in an eye that was structurally integrated but unable to see, largely because the optic nerve connection wasn't re-established. The new device apparently addresses the preservation problem by maintaining the eye in a more viable state for longer, giving surgeons more time to work and potentially enabling better surgical outcomes. Restoring the optic nerve connection remains the core scientific challenge, and researchers are investigating whether techniques used in other nerve regeneration contexts could be adapted. The potential patient population includes people who lost eyes to injury or disease where the visual cortex and optic pathway are intact — restoring sight in these cases would be transformative. This is early-stage research, but it's a credible step toward something that would have seemed like science fiction a decade ago.

stories.title: Three nuclear startups hit reactor milestones, but meaningful energy scale is still years away
stories.summary: Three nuclear energy startups are celebrating new reactor design milestones around the Fourth of July, with Wired providing a characteristically measured assessment: the milestones are real and meaningful, but commercial power delivery at scale is still substantially further off than the companies' PR suggests. The nuclear startup ecosystem has been energized by AI companies' enormous power needs and the recognition that zero-carbon baseload power is something renewables plus storage struggle to provide economically. Advanced reactor designs — including small modular reactors and various Generation IV concepts — promise to be cheaper, safer, and more flexible than traditional large-scale nuclear plants. The milestones being celebrated likely involve regulatory approvals, first criticality achievements, or construction starts — the article unfortunately doesn't specify which companies or exactly what they achieved. What's important to understand is that nuclear timelines are long even when everything goes right: from milestone to grid-connected power typically takes a decade or more. The gap between 'reactor design approved' and 'gigawatts on the grid' is enormous. That said, getting design approvals and early hardware milestones done now is genuinely necessary for nuclear to contribute to the 2035-2040 power needs that AI infrastructure is creating. Progress is real; the hype still needs calibration.

stories.title: AI-driven chip shortage is pushing consumer electronics prices higher — again
stories.summary: Wired reports that consumer electronics prices are rising again across phones, computers, and gaming consoles, this time driven by an AI-induced chip shortage rather than the pandemic supply chain disruptions that characterized 2021-2022. The mechanism is straightforward: the enormous demand for AI accelerators — primarily Nvidia GPUs and custom AI chips — is consuming semiconductor fab capacity and driving up the cost of advanced process nodes, which in turn raises costs for chips used in consumer devices. TSMC and Samsung's leading-edge fabs are heavily allocated to AI chip production, leaving less capacity for the display drivers, application processors, and other chips that go into phones and laptops. Unlike the pandemic shortage, which was a supply shock, this is a demand-driven shortage with a self-reinforcing quality: as AI applications become more capable and valuable, demand for AI chips grows, further crowding out consumer silicon. The practical implication for consumers is that the upgrade cycle assumptions that made sense two years ago no longer apply — prices aren't falling the way they used to, and the expectation that next year's model will be meaningfully cheaper than this year's is less reliable. For enterprise buyers, this affects hardware refresh planning in meaningful ways. It's also worth noting that this dynamic creates a perverse incentive structure where consumer hardware quality improvements slow down because margins are being squeezed.
